title: INNOVATIVE THINKING, INFINITE HUMOR: HUMOR RESEARCH OF LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS THROUGH STRUCTURED THOUGHT LEAPS
venue: Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2025
research_questions: Can injecting and augmenting external knowledge into humorous conversation data mitigate sparse knowledge-graph paths and improve multi-hop humor reasoning?
Can judgment-oriented supervised fine-tuning plus Direct Preference Optimization with GPT-4o-extracted rationales improve LLM humor judgment and generation?
How does LoL compare with GPT-4o, Qwen, LLaMA3, CLoT, QwQ, and other baselines on Chinese and English humor judgment tasks and humor generation preference tests?

datasets_used.name: Ruozhiba dataset
datasets_used.size: first 200 samples from the validation subset for the user study

methods_proposed.name: LoL
methods_proposed.summary: A two-stage framework for humor reasoning that first trains judgment and understanding abilities with supervised fine-tuning, then improves generation through Direct Preference Optimization with rationales.

methods_proposed.name: Automatic Instruction Expansion (AIE)
methods_proposed.summary: A three-agent instruction-evolution system that uses a generator, imaginator, and selector/critic to enrich seed humorous conversations with broader and deeper contextual knowledge while controlling over-divergence from the original joke.

methods_proposed.name: Judgement-oriented instruction templates
methods_proposed.summary: Human-designed templates train selection, ranking, answer rewriting, and teacher-student judgment skills from question-answer data with human-voting annotations.

methods_proposed.name: Teacher-Student Prompt Loop
methods_proposed.summary: Two GPT-4o agents simulate trial-and-error, reflection, and backtracking: a teacher gives feedback and hints while a student revises answers until the response aligns with the ideal humorous answer or reaches a step limit.

methods_proposed.name: Guided Explorative Self-Improvement Tuning (GESIT)
methods_proposed.summary: An online DPO training procedure where the SFT model is copied into a frozen judge and a trainable policy; sampled response pairs are judged, rationales are generated by GPT-4o, and the augmented preference data are mixed back into training.

methods_proposed.name: LoL inference step
methods_proposed.summary: At inference time, the model generates multiple candidate responses from sampled prompts and then uses its own judgment ability to select the funniest answer.

evaluation_methods.detail: Top-1 accuracy / acc% on single-choice humor judgment tasks: 2T1, 2T1(hard), 3T1, and 4T1.
Human-voting annotations and Likes from humor games and communities are used to construct positive/negative pairs and choice-question labels.
Divergent Association Task (DAT) scores are computed with Chinese Word Vectors to evaluate associative generalization and divergent thinking.
User-preference study over four LLMs on 200 text-based questions; the paper reports 15 valid questionnaires and 3000 votes.
Three experts assessed logical coherence of responses on 200 examples from GPT-4o, GPT-4, and QWEN2-57B.

headline_results.finding: On diverse English benchmarks, LoL’s average accuracy increased over LLAMA3-70B and GPT-4o.
On diverse Chinese benchmarks, LoL’s average accuracy increased over GPT-4o.
On English choice questions, OURS-32B achieved the best score on five of six columns; GPT-4o was best only on SemEval 2021 2T1(hard).
headline_results.dataset: HaHackathon (SemEval-2021 Task 7); Humicroedit + FunLines (SemEval-2020 Task 7); Oogiri-GO

headline_results.finding: On Chinese choice questions, OURS-32B was best on both reported benchmark tasks.
On the ruozhiba 2T1 validation, LoL achieved the top score, ahead of QwQ and GPT-4o.
In ablations, ALL+AIE(DIET) substantially improved over QWEN1.5-32B on Oogiri-GO-en and SemEval 2021 3T1.

key_findings: LoL improves humor judgment on nearly all reported English and Chinese choice-question benchmarks, with especially large gains on Chinese 2T1 tasks.
Judgment-oriented data construction, especially teacher-student feedback and AIE, is presented as important for training models to distinguish humorous from non-humorous answers.
The paper reports that AIE improves divergent associative thinking in DAT validation and that LoL has the highest stabilized DAT score among compared systems.
Guided Explorative Self-Improvement Tuning is reported to strengthen the logical coherence of generated answers in expert evaluation.
In the human preference study, users were reported to have a strong inclination to select LoL outputs as the most creative and humorous among LoL, GPT-4o, QWEN1.5-32B, and CLoT.
limitations: The paper states that humor is subjective and that a unified score may contain significant noise from topics and background.
The failure-case analysis says the level of creativity is uneven because creativity in the training samples varies significantly.
Some human-preferred responses beat LoL in the failure cases, showing that LoL’s outputs are not always the most preferred for all users.
The ablation discussion notes that using AIE only on Oogiri-GO-en caused a slight decrease in some performance, possibly due to overfitting to divergence of thought in that dataset.
The text reports human preference trends but the transcribed paper text does not provide exact numeric win-rate values from Figure 6(b) or Figure 6(c).

human_samples.population: Original SemEval 2021 Task 7 annotators/raters; paper says labels and ratings were collected from a balanced age group ranging from 18 to 70 years old.
human_samples.observations: Binary labels and ratings used by this paper to construct easy-case and hard-case humor judgment tasks.
human_samples.detail: The paper does not report the number of SemEval 2021 annotators or ratings in this study.

human_samples.population: Original SemEval 2020 Task 7 labelers/raters for edited news headlines.
human_samples.observations: Binary labels and ratings used to construct easy-case and hard-case humor judgment tasks.
human_samples.detail: The paper reports use of the dataset’s labels/ratings but not annotator counts or demographics.

human_samples.population: Oogiri game participants and voters from the Oogiri-GO source dataset.
human_samples.observations: Human-voting annotations/Likes are used to identify humorous responses and construct training/test judgment data; the paper randomly uses 95% of Oogiri-GO samples for training and 5% for testing.
human_samples.detail: The paper describes Oogiri game participants but does not report participant counts, vote counts, or sample counts.

human_samples.population: Users of Chinese communities, including Ruozhiba and others.
human_samples.observations: Community human-voting/Likes labels are used to construct easy-case and hard-case Chinese humor tasks; the first 200 samples from the Ruozhiba validation subset are used for the generation user study.
human_samples.detail: The paper does not report numbers of community contributors, voters, Likes, or total Chinese Community Data items.

human_samples.population: Users completing an online preference survey.
human_samples.observations: 15 valid questionnaires with 3000 votes over 200 questions; users chose the most creative and humorous response among four LLM outputs whose options were randomly arranged.
human_samples.detail: The paper reports questionnaires and votes, but does not explicitly state a participant count, demographics, geography, or whether respondents were blind to model identity.

human_samples.observations: Logical-coherence assessments on 200 examples from GPT-4o, GPT-4, and QWEN2-57B; wording does not clarify whether 200 is total or per model.
human_samples.detail: Used to evaluate GESIT/logical reasoning according to the main text. Blinding, annotation rubric details, and inter-annotator agreement are not reported.

judges.name: SemEval 2021 and SemEval 2020 dataset annotators/raters
judges.detail: Their binary labels and ratings are used to form positive/negative choices and easy/hard humor judgment tasks.

judges.name: Community Likes / human-voting annotations
judges.detail: Human-voting data from humor games and communities are transformed into pairwise data, ranking/selection labels, and Chinese benchmark labels.

judges.name: GPT-4o
judges.detail: Used to rewrite the most humorous answers into non-humorous versions; two GPT-4o agents are used in the teacher-student prompt loop; GPT-4o rewrites Ruozhiba queries into question-answer pairs and rewrites ground-truth answers into non-humorous versions; GPT-4o is also the causal-inference expert that generates rationales for GESIT preference pairs.

judges.name: LoL first-stage frozen model / π*
judges.detail: During GESIT, the frozen copy of the SFT model judges two sampled policy responses and assigns the more humorous one as positive and the other as negative.

judges.name: LoL-trained LLM / π′
judges.detail: At inference time, Algorithm 3 uses the trained model’s judgment ability to select the most funny answer from multiple generated candidates.

Automatic Instruction Expansion uses generator, imaginator, and selector/critic agents plus criteria to produce and filter evolved instruction-output data; the implementing LLM(s) and versions are not specified.

judges.name: Top-1 accuracy / acc%
judges.detail: Determines reported performance on 2T1, 2T1(hard), 3T1, and 4T1 humor judgment choice tasks against constructed labels.

judges.name: Divergent Association Task score using Chinese Word Vectors
judges.detail: Used to evaluate associative generalization/divergent thinking from generated associated words.

judges.name: Online user-preference survey respondents
judges.detail: Users choose the most creative and humorous response among four LLM outputs for 200 Ruozhiba-derived questions; options are randomly arranged, but model-identity blinding is not stated.

judges.name: Three experts
judges.detail: Main text says three experts assessed logical coherence of responses on 200 examples from GPT-4o, GPT-4, and QWEN2-57B.

judges.name: GPT-4 / GPT-4o
judges.detail: Figure 6 caption states that GPT-4 and GPT-4o logically evaluate model outputs after DIET and LoL, which conflicts with or is ambiguous relative to the main-text claim that three experts were enlisted.

generation_selection.scope: LoL final humor/witty-response inference, including the inference algorithm used for generation showcases and likely LoL generation outputs
generation_selection.n: n prompts / n different answers; numeric n not reported
generation_selection.detail: Algorithm 3 selects n prompts from a prompt set, generates n candidate responses, and then uses the LoL-trained model itself as a judgment model to select the most funny answer.

generation_selection.scope: GESIT online preference-pair augmentation during DPO training
generation_selection.detail: The trainable policy samples two answers; the frozen first-stage model π* judges which is more humorous to construct positive/negative preference data.

generation_selection.n: maximum 3 evolution rounds, not a reported number of alternative candidates
generation_selection.detail: Generator/imaginator/selector-critic agents and Criterion-1/Criterion-2 filter evolved instructions and imagined chats; the number of sampled alternatives, model identity, and sampling parameters are not reported.

generation_selection.scope: GPT-4o rewriting for answer-rewriting data and Ruozhiba benchmark construction
generation_selection.detail: The paper says GPT-4o rewrites most humorous answers into non-humorous versions and rewrites Ruozhiba queries/ground-truth answers; no multiple-candidate selection is described.
For each supplied word, the model is asked to generate associations/imagination and obtain 10 associated words; no best-of selection is described.

generation_selection.scope: Human preference study outputs from GPT-4o, QWEN1.5-32B, and CLoT
generation_selection.detail: The paper says four LLMs generate responses for 200 questions and the four responses are presented as options, but it does not state whether non-LoL baselines used single samples, multiple samples, or any selection procedure.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4o
model_provenance.detail: Used as a baseline model, as a rewriting model, in teacher-student data construction, as a rationale generator/causal inference expert, and for Ruozhiba data transformation; no API snapshot, release date, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4
model_provenance.detail: Mentioned in the logical-coherence/GESIT evaluation context; no snapshot or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: LLAMA3-8B
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated on English and Chinese humor judgment benchmarks; instruct/chat variant and checkpoint are not specified.

model_provenance.name: LLAMA3-70B
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated on English and Chinese humor judgment benchmarks; instruct/chat variant and checkpoint are not specified.

model_provenance.name: QWEN1.5-7B
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as a benchmark baseline; exact checkpoint is not specified.

model_provenance.name: QWEN1.5-14B
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as a benchmark baseline; exact checkpoint is not specified.

model_provenance.name: QWEN1.5-32B
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as a benchmark and generation-study baseline; exact checkpoint is not specified.

model_provenance.name: QWEN1.5-32B-Chat
model_provenance.detail: Implementation details state LoL is mainly validated based on QWEN1.5-32B-Chat with LoRA on 8 A100 GPUs; no checkpoint hash or model release snapshot is reported.

model_provenance.name: QWEN2-7B
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as a benchmark baseline; exact checkpoint is not specified.

model_provenance.name: QWEN2-57B
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as a benchmark baseline and appears in the logical-coherence evaluation context; exact checkpoint is not specified.

model_provenance.name: QWEN2.5-32B
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as a benchmark baseline; exact checkpoint is not specified.

model_provenance.name: Baichuan2-13B
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as a benchmark baseline; exact checkpoint is not specified.

model_provenance.name: CLoT-7B
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as a benchmark and generation-study baseline; exact checkpoint is not specified.

model_provenance.name: QwQ-32B
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated on English, Chinese, and Ruozhiba judgment tasks; table does not specify preview/snapshot.

model_provenance.name: QwQ+LoL-32B
model_provenance.detail: Reported in the Chinese benchmark table; training or checkpoint details are not provided.

model_provenance.name: LoL / OURS-32B
model_provenance.detail: Authors’ trained model, based mainly on QWEN1.5-32B-Chat with LoRA; no released checkpoint, hash, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: LoL/QWEN1.5-32B ablation variants
model_provenance.detail: Ablation configurations include OOGIRI-GO+Figure 2(C), OOGIRI-GO+Figure 2(C,D), OOGIRI-GO+AIE, ALL+Figure 2(C,D), ALL+AIE(DIET), +Figure 2(C), +Figure 2(C)(D), and +AIE(DIET); exact checkpoints are not reported.

inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: temperature=1; top k=50; top p=1.0; length penalty=1.0
inference_budgets.detail: Table 6 reports these as validation parameters, but the paper does not map them to every model/task or report max tokens.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: n generation calls plus one self-judgment selection call, as described symbolically
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: n candidate responses; numeric n not reported
inference_budgets.detail: The model randomly selects n prompts from a predefined prompt set, generates n answers, and selects A_best itself; prompt set and n are not specified.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: when triggered: two policy generations, one frozen-model comparison, and two GPT-4o rationale generations per sampled question
inference_budgets.detail: Triggered every T steps for l questions from D0, but T and l are not numerically reported.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: up to 3 evolutionary rounds with generator and imaginator calls; analyst/rule update may be invoked
inference_budgets.detail: Algorithm 1 specifies while t<3 and criteria-based stopping; it does not report model identity, prompt-call counts under each branch, or sampling settings.

inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 10 associated words generated for each target word, then scored with the target word as 11 words total
inference_budgets.detail: Number of target words/domains and model-call counts are not reported in the text.

inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 4 presented responses per question, one from each of LoL, GPT-4o, QWEN1.5-32B, and CLoT
inference_budgets.detail: 200 questions are used; the paper does not report generation prompts, max tokens, candidate counts for baselines, or whether LoL’s best-of-n inference was used.

Stage 1 SFT: 6 epochs, AdamW, learning rate 3e-4, using 95% of the dataset mentioned above. Stage 2 GESIT/DPO: 3 epochs, AdamW, learning rate 2e-4, using 5% of the dataset; batches mix with-rationale and without-rationale data at 1:1.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: GPT-4o is asked to rewrite queries into question-answer pairs and then asked again to rewrite ground-truth answers into non-humorous versions
inference_budgets.detail: Exact call counts, prompts beyond the description, and sampling parameters are not reported.

human_baselines.description: Models are scored against labels/ratings/votes, but no human accuracy baseline on the constructed 2T1/2T1(hard)/3T1/4T1 tasks is reported.
human_baselines.detail: Human annotations provide ground truth, not a reported human-performance comparator.

human_baselines.description: The preference options are LoL, GPT-4o, QWEN1.5-32B, and CLoT outputs; no human-written response option is included as a baseline.
human_baselines.detail: Humans judge model outputs, but human-generated humor is not evaluated under matched conditions.

human_baselines.scope: DAT creativity validation and logical-coherence validation
human_baselines.description: DAT compares model-generated associated words; logical-coherence validation compares model responses before/after methods or across models. No human generative baseline is reported.
human_baselines.detail: Experts serve as evaluators, not as a human baseline system.

mitigations: Oogiri-GO is split randomly into 95% training samples and a remaining 5% test set.
The user study uses the Ruozhiba validation subset, specifically the first 200 samples.
contamination_risk.basis: The study evaluates later LLMs on public legacy datasets including SemEval 2020, SemEval 2021, Oogiri-GO, and Ruozhiba-derived items. The paper explicitly states that the Ruozhiba dataset is one that most well-known LLMs have been trained on. LoL itself is trained on public/community humor data and evaluated on held-out/random or validation portions, but no decontamination test, private benchmark, or convincing pretraining-contamination control is reported. GPT-4o also rewrites some evaluation items, which changes format but does not establish decontamination.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: Core method and some evaluations depend on LLM judgments or LLM-constructed labels: GPT-4o non-humorous rewrites, teacher-student feedback, AIE filtering, GESIT preference labels/rationales, Ruozhiba 2T1 construction, and LoL best-of-n self-selection for generation. Main SemEval/Oogiri accuracy scoring and the user-preference votes are not themselves primarily LLM-judged.
llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: The paper reports a human preference survey with 15 valid questionnaires/3000 votes and a three-expert logical-coherence assessment, and benchmark labels originate from human labels/votes. It does not report human validation of GPT-4o rationale quality, GPT-4o non-humorous rewrites, AIE filtering decisions, or the frozen model’s preference labels.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: LLMs are not merely diagnostic tools: they create training data, construct some positive/negative evaluation pairs, generate rationales for DPO data, and select final LoL generation outputs. Therefore LLM-judge dependence is substantial rather than none/secondary, even though headline benchmark accuracy is computed automatically against human-derived labels.

reporting_gaps: No API snapshots, exact checkpoint hashes, release snapshots, or inference dates are reported for GPT-4o, GPT-4, QwQ, LLaMA3, Qwen, Baichuan2, CLoT, or LoL variants.
The paper does not report total dataset sizes for most constructed training/test sets, item counts for the Chinese benchmarks, or exact counts for SemEval-derived easy/hard tasks.
User study reports 15 valid questionnaires and 3000 votes but not an explicit participant count, respondent demographics, geography, recruitment criteria, compensation, or model-identity blinding.
Human preference win-rate values in Figure 6(b) and Figure 6(c) are not numerically provided in the transcribed text.
Generation settings for baseline models in the human study are not reported, including prompts, temperature, max tokens, number of candidates, and any selection/cherry-picking procedure.
LoL inference uses n candidate responses and self-selection, but numeric n, prompt pool P, and token limits are not reported.
GESIT reports symbolic T and l for online augmentation but does not give numeric values for the frequency of augmentation or the number of questions sampled per augmentation step.
AIE does not report the concrete LLM(s), versions, sampling parameters, or full selection criteria implementation for generator/imaginator/selector agents.
The logical-coherence evaluation is ambiguous: the main text says three experts were enlisted, while the Figure 6 caption says GPT-4 and GPT-4o logically evaluate outputs.
No inter-annotator agreement, expert rubric, or blinding details are reported for the three-expert logical-coherence assessment.
No formal pretraining-contamination/decontamination analysis is reported despite public benchmarks and the paper’s statement that Ruozhiba is likely in many LLMs’ training data.
Training data overlap controls between the 95% SFT split, the 5% test/validation split, GESIT data, and public benchmark items are not fully specified.
Costs, wall-clock time, batch sizes, LoRA ranks, maximum sequence lengths, and decoding max-token limits are not reported.
